justification: All rubric criteria met: (1) Both training and serving pipelines designed in detail — Training Pipeline as weekly batch job with feature engineering, model training/validation, and versioned artifact publishing; Prediction Service as stateless in-memory model server. (2) 100ms P99 constraint explicitly addressed as a hard constraint with multiple mitigations: in-process feature computation, in-memory model loading, XGBoost/LightGBM for sub-5ms inference, cached external signals with timeouts and fallbacks. (3) Feature store/shared feature library identified as necessary for both latency and train-serve consistency. (4) Model versioning with full promotion lifecycle (candidate → active → archived) and rollback mechanism via Model Registry and Promotion Controller. (5) Team size factored throughout — managed services recommended, custom infra explicitly warned against, phased implementation plan sized for 4 engineers with clear ownership split. (6) Trade-offs and risks thoroughly documented with severity levels and mitigations.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough two-path architecture with clear separation between training (cold) and serving (hot) paths
100ms P99 constraint treated as a hard invariant with multiple layers of protection: in-process features, in-memory model, cached external signals with fallback defaults, circuit breakers
Shared feature library pattern explicitly chosen to prevent train-serve skew, identified as the #1 ML production bug class
Model registry with full promotion lifecycle (candidate → active → archived) with shadow scoring validation before promotion
Strong team-size awareness: managed services recommended, custom ML platform explicitly warned against, realistic phased rollout plan
Model complexity guidance provided — XGBoost/LightGBM chosen over neural nets specifically to meet latency constraints, with rationale
Risk of feature store/cache staleness explicitly identified with mitigations (TTLs, fallback defaults, distribution drift monitoring)
Model drift risk addressed: real-time prediction distribution monitoring plus post-delivery accuracy tracking
Went beyond requirements with domain model, bounded contexts, flexibility/rigidity points, and phased implementation guidance

agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Architecture for a real-time ML delivery time estimation service serving predictions at sub-100ms P99 latency during checkout. The design separates the hot prediction path (a lean inference service with pre-loaded models) from the cold training path (a weekly batch pipeline that produces versioned model artifacts). A model registry with promotion/rollback semantics gives the business safe deployments. The system is sized for a 4-person team—pragmatic, not over-engineered—us
